title: A Syntomic Perspective on Integral Canonical Models
speaker: Alex Youcis
abstract:
Since Langlands's earliest paper on his now famous program, canonical integral models of Shimura varieties have occupied a central role in modern number theory. Steady progress has been made in the intervening 50 years toward the correct formulation of the notion of 'canonical', the study of the remarkable properties of such models, and ultimately of their construction. In this talk, I will discuss work with Madapusi (continuing previous work of Imai--Kato--Youcis in the abelian-type setting) on giving a new, and what we believe to be more robust, notion of 'canonical' using recent advances in p
-adic Hodge theory due to Bhatt--Scholze, Drinfeld, Bhatt--Lurie, and others. In particular, we give constructions of models of pre-abelian type Shimura varieties for primes p greater than 2, extending and more conceptually framing previous constructions of Kisin in the abelian-type setting. We moreover recapture the models for arbitrary Shimura varieties for p≫0 recently obtained by Bakker--Shankar--Tsimerman from this perspective. Finally, we discuss how this new notion of canonicity developed here provides the correct foundations to prove many strong results about such models, including Néron-type mapping properties and algebraization questions of the following kind: which p-divisible groups over 𝔽⎯⎯⎯p arise as A[p∞] for an abelian variety A?
tags: Workshop on Recent Developments in Hodge Theory and O-minimality

proof_stages.label: Siegel and p-divisible-group template
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with the Siegel moduli space of principally polarized abelian varieties, its map to the stack of principally polarized p-divisible groups, Serre-Tate formal étaleness, and the André-Vasiu mapping criterion showing that extension of maps to the integral model is controlled by local p-adic data.

proof_stages.label: Generic Shimura-level torsor and the missing integral objects
proof_stages.summary: For a Shimura datum with hyperspecial level at p, varying the p-level gives a generic G(Z_p)-torsor and hence a map to B G(Z_p), but the speaker explains that the integral analogue cannot be a naive moduli of p-divisible groups with G-structure because such G-structures require unavailable linear algebra operations on p-divisible groups.

proof_stages.label: Syntomic p-divisible-group analogue BT_{G,mu}
proof_stages.summary: The replacement for p-divisible groups with G-structure is introduced as the Drinfeld-Gardner-Madapusi functor of G-bundles of type mu on the syntomification X_syn, with intuition from p-adic Hodge theory and the theorem that it is a pro-smooth formal Artin stack.

proof_stages.label: Algebraization of the local stack
proof_stages.summary: The formal syntomic stack is algebraized to a pro-smooth Artin-Scholze stack BT_{G,mu}^{alg,∞} whose generic fiber is B G, giving the desired integral right-hand column, and the Q&A explains its Beauville-Laszlo-style gluing construction.

proof_stages.label: Definition of syntomic integral canonical models
proof_stages.summary: A separated finite-type model S_K is defined to be a syntomic integral canonical model when it admits a formally étale map to BT_{G,mu}^{alg,∞} extending the generic torsor map and when the generic fiber of its completion is exactly the crystalline locus; the additional limpid condition imposes unramifiedness of the prime-to-p local systems.

proof_stages.label: Existence and functorial propagation of limpid models
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states existence of limpid integral canonical models for abelian type, pre-abelian type for p>2, and arbitrary Shimura data for p sufficiently large, explaining that limpidness is inherited by closed Shimura subvarieties and ascends/descends along central covers, thereby streamlining and extending Kisin-style constructions.

proof_stages.label: Mapping, Néron, and surjectivity consequences
proof_stages.summary: The syntomic definition yields a general mapping property analogous to André-Vasiu, identifies the formal completion as the Néron model of the crystalline locus so that the integral model is obtained by gluing along that locus, and gives a generalized Oort-type surjectivity theorem for the special fiber map to the local stack.

proof_stages.label: Further structures and future directions
proof_stages.summary: The final part surveys further outputs and prospects, including Serre-Tate coordinates and canonical lifts, G-shtukas and p-isogeny stacks, Langlands-Rapoport point parameterizations, logarithmic/toroidal compactifications, and limitations related to minuscule geometry.

examples.name: Oort's mod-p surjectivity in the Siegel case
examples.note: The speaker uses Oort's theorem that the reduction of the map from principally polarized abelian varieties to principally polarized p-divisible groups is surjective as an optional demonstration of how formal étaleness, partial properness, and connectedness give useful global conclusions from the local p-adic picture.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker not only states Oort's mod-p surjectivity but sketches a proof via formal étaleness giving openness, partial properness giving closedness/specializing, and connectedness implying surjectivity.

passes.reason: The Siegel mod-p morphism has maximal possible image, namely all of the p-divisible-group stack, and is used as a positive template.

examples.name: Twistor projective line analogy
examples.note: The nontrivial Brauer-Severi form of the projective line over the reals is recalled because vector bundles on it encode real Hodge structures, illustrating by analogy how bundles on the syntomification encode p-adic Hodge-theoretic data.
examples.engagement_reason: The twistor projective line is used as an analogy for syntomification, with only descriptive facts about vector bundles/Hodge structures stated.

examples.name: Supersingular elliptic curve deformation
examples.note: A supersingular elliptic curve over an algebraic closure of F_p and its universal deformation over W[[t]] are used to make the syntomic construction concrete via an explicit rank-two Breuil-Kisin-style module with Frobenius matrix.
examples.engagement_reason: The supersingular elliptic curve is concretely run through universal deformation and syntomic Dieudonné theory, reducing the data to a rank-two module with a Frobenius matrix.

examples.name: Exceptional E7 matrices
examples.note: The speaker notes that the same local syntomic description can be written for an exceptional group such as E7, only with much larger, e.g. 56-by-56, matrices, illustrating that the construction is not special to GL_2 or elliptic curves.

examples.name: Shtukas with non-minuscule bound
examples.note: In Q&A, moduli of shtukas are invoked as an analogy: minuscule bounds give rigid spaces, while non-minuscule bounds lead to diamonds, illustrating why the syntomic stack has good classical geometry mainly in the minuscule Shimura-variety range.
examples.engagement_reason: Non-minuscule shtukas are used only as an analogy, with the stated contrast that minuscule bounds give rigid spaces while non-minuscule ones give diamonds.
